steps.title: Ensure Scene Safety
steps.detail: Before approaching any accident scene, scan for hazards: oncoming traffic, fuel leaks, fire, or unstable vehicles. Turn on your hazard lights and if safe, place warning triangles or flares 50–100 metres behind the scene. Do not become a second victim.
doList: Check for fuel leaks or fire
dontList: Move the vehicle unless blocking traffic

steps.title: Call Emergency Services
steps.summary: Call 112 immediately — stay on the line
steps.detail: Call the emergency number for your country immediately (112 is the universal number). Tell the operator: your exact location or nearest landmark, the number of vehicles involved, estimated number of injured persons, and any hazards present. Stay on the line — the operator will guide you.

steps.title: Assess Casualties
steps.detail: Approach each person carefully. Tap their shoulders firmly and shout 'Can you hear me?'. If unconscious but breathing, place in the recovery position. If not breathing normally, begin CPR. Do not move casualties unless they are in immediate danger from fire or water.
dontList: Move a casualty with a suspected spinal injury
Remove crash helmets unless airway is blocked

steps.title: Apply Direct Pressure
steps.summary: Press firmly with a clean cloth
steps.detail: Use a clean cloth, bandage, or clothing to apply firm, direct pressure to the wound. Press continuously for at least 10 minutes without lifting to check — lifting disrupts clot formation. If blood soaks through, add more material on top without removing the first layer.
doList: Add more material over the first
dontList: Apply a tourniquet unless bleeding is life-threatening and on a limb

steps.title: Tourniquet (Last Resort)
steps.summary: Only for limb bleeding that cannot be controlled
steps.detail: A tourniquet is only appropriate for severe, uncontrolled bleeding on an arm or leg. Apply 5–8 cm above the wound, tighten until bleeding stops, and note the time of application. Improvise with a belt or strip of cloth and a stick to twist and tighten. Write the time on the person's forehead if possible.
dontList: Use a tourniquet on the neck, chest, or abdomen

steps.title: Internal Bleeding
steps.summary: Recognise the signs of hidden bleeding
steps.detail: Internal bleeding is common in high-impact accidents. Watch for: bruising, swelling, or pain in the abdomen; vomiting blood; bleeding from ears, nose, or mouth; signs of shock without visible bleeding. Keep the person warm, still, and calm while waiting for professional help.
doList: Check for bruising on the chest or abdomen
Keep the person calm and warm
dontList: Allow the person to walk around

steps.title: Check for Breathing
steps.summary: Look, listen, and feel for 10 seconds
steps.detail: Tilt the head back gently and lift the chin to open the airway. Look for chest movement, listen for breath sounds, and feel for air on your cheek. Do this for no more than 10 seconds. If the person is not breathing normally, start CPR immediately.
doList: Begin CPR if no normal breathing
dontList: Begin CPR if the person is breathing normally

steps.title: Chest Compressions
steps.detail: Place the heel of your hand on the centre of the chest (lower half of the breastbone). Place your other hand on top and interlock fingers. Compress the chest at least 5–6 cm deep at a rate of 100–120 per minute. Allow full chest recoil between compressions. Count aloud: 1-and-2-and-3...
dontList: Lean on the chest between compressions
Stop compressions for more than 10 seconds

steps.title: Rescue Breaths (if trained)
steps.detail: If trained and willing, give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions. Pinch the nose, seal your mouth over theirs, and breathe steadily for 1 second watching for chest rise. If untrained or unwilling, hands-only CPR (compressions only) is still highly effective. Continue until emergency services arrive.
dontList: Stop CPR unless exhausted or a professional takes over
Attempt rescue breaths if not trained

steps.title: Recognise Shock
steps.summary: Pale, cold, rapid breathing — act quickly
steps.detail: Signs of shock include: pale, cold, clammy skin; rapid, weak pulse; fast, shallow breathing; confusion or anxiety; nausea. Shock is life-threatening and worsens rapidly without treatment. Call emergency services immediately if not already done.
doList: Raise legs 30cm unless head, neck, or chest injury
dontList: Apply heat or hot water bottles

steps.title: Keep Warm and Still
steps.summary: Prevent heat loss and further injury
steps.detail: Cover the person with a blanket, coat, or any available material to retain body heat. Do not apply direct heat. Keep them lying still and reassure them calmly. Monitor breathing and pulse continuously until emergency services arrive.
dontList: Raise legs if head, neck, or spinal injury suspected

steps.title: Do Not Move
steps.summary: Immobility is critical — movement can paralyse
steps.detail: If a spinal injury is suspected (high-speed impact, fall from height, unconscious casualty, complaint of neck or back pain, numbness or tingling in limbs), do not move the person. Keep them absolutely still. If conscious, tell them not to move their head or neck. Only move if in immediate, life-threatening danger.
doList: Talk calmly to keep them conscious
Support the head in the position found
dontList: Remove a crash helmet unless airway is blocked

steps.title: Manual Stabilisation
steps.summary: Hold the head still and in line
steps.detail: If you must wait for a long time, use your hands to gently support the person's head on either side, keeping it in line with their spine. Do not try to straighten the neck; just hold it in the position you found it. Reassure the person and tell them not to nod or shake their head in response to questions.
doList: Hold the head gently with both hands
Keep the head in line with the spine
Ask 'yes/no' questions to prevent head movement
dontList: Use a neck collar if not trained

steps.title: Cool the Burn
steps.detail: Hold the burned area under cool (not cold) running water for at least 20 minutes. This reduces heat in the tissue and limits damage. Do not use ice, ice water, butter, toothpaste, or any cream. Remove jewellery and clothing near the burn unless stuck to the skin.
doList: Cool with running water for 20 minutes
Cover loosely with cling film or a clean plastic bag
